steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: For coordinate-circle questions, read the center and radius from the equation or graph the circle as a check. For diagram facts, mark equal radii, diameters, tangents, and arcs before doing algebra.

steps.title: Identify complementary arcs and use the arc length formula
steps.content:
Arc ADC and arc ABC are two different paths from point A to point C around the circle. Together, they form the complete circumference: Arc ADC + Arc ABC = 2πr. We're given arc ADC = 5π. The angle x = 100° represents the central angle that corresponds to arc ADC.

Using the arc length formula $\text{Arc length} = \frac{\text{central angle}}{360^{\circ}} \cdot 2\pi r$, we can find the radius:
$$5\pi = \frac{100^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}} \cdot 2\pi r$$

steps.title: Solve for the radius and circumference
steps.content:
Simplify the equation:
$$5\pi = \frac{100}{360} \cdot 2\pi r = \frac{5}{18} \cdot 2\pi r$$
Divide both sides by $\pi$:
$$5 = \frac{5}{18} \cdot 2r = \frac{10r}{18}$$
Multiply both sides by $\frac{18}{10}$:
$$r = 5 \cdot \frac{18}{10} = 9$$
So the circumference is:
$$2\pi r = 2\pi(9) = 18\pi$$

steps.title: Find arc ABC using complementary arcs
steps.content:
Since arc ADC and arc ABC together form the complete circle:
$$\text{Arc } ABC = \text{Circumference} - \text{Arc } ADC = 18\pi - 5\pi = 13\pi$$
That is the correct answer.
